text: CONFIDENTIAL
April 30, 2001
To: Members of the Executive Board
From: The Secretary
Subject:

Chad—Second Review Under the Three-Year Arrangement Under the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility, Second Annual Program, and Requests for an Increase of the Arrangement and for a Waiver and Modification of Performance Criteria

Attached for consideration by the Executive Directors is a paper on the second review under the three-year arrangement under the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility with Chad, its second annual program, and Chad's requests for an increase of the arrangement and for a waiver and modification of performance criteria, which is tentatively scheduled for discussion on Monday, May 14, 2001. A draft decision appears on pages 22-25. At the time of circulation of this paper to the Board, the Secretary's Department has not received a communication from the authorities of Chad indicating whether or not they consent to the Fund's publication of this paper; such communication may be received after the authorities have had an opportunity to read the paper.
Questions may be referred to Ms. Nagy (ext. 34029), Mr. Karangwa (ext. 38612) and Mr. Dauphin (ext. 39705).

Unless the Documents Section (ext. 36760) is otherwise notified, the document will be transmitted, in accordance with the procedures approved by the Executive Board and with the appropriate deletions, to the WTO Secretariat on Tuesday, May 8, 200l; and to the African Development Bank, the European Commission, and the Islamic Development Bank, following its consideration by the Executive Board.

INTERNATIONALMONETARYFUND
CHAD

Second Review Under the Three-Year Arrangement Under the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility, Second Annual Program, and Request for an Increase of the Arrangement and for a Waiver and Modification of Performance Criteria
Prepared by the African Department
(In consultation with Fiscal, Legal, Monetary and Exchange Affairs, Policy Development and Review, Statistics, and Treasurer's Departments)
Approved by Amor Tahari and Michael T. Hadjimichael
April 30, 2001
EXECUTIVE SUMMARY

Chad's economic performance under the first annual PRGF-supported program has been uneven. After a broadly satisfactory performance in the first half of 2oo0, policy slippages in the second half led to a delay in the completion of the second PRGF review, as well as in reaching the HIPC decision point. The main reasons were governance problems and fiscal slippage. As a result of corrective measures taken since November 2000, Chad's track record has improved. All prior actions for the HIPC decision point and the completion of the discussions on the second PRGF review (with exception of the end-December fiscal target) have been fulfilled.   
The proposed second annual PRGF-supported program has two main components: fiscal adjustment and a package of governance measures, including several audits. The program also provides for a series of structural fiscal measures and a liberalization of domestic petroleum prices; other structural areas are covered by World Bank conditionality. The new annual program incorporates quarterly program monitoring.   
In the face of a famine threat, the international community is providing food aid;   
nevertheless, a significant balance of payments need remains uncovered, for which the Chadian government requests an increase of the arrangement by SDR 5.6 million   
(corresponding to 10 percent of quota).   
The staff appraisal $\because$ (i) highlights the improved track record after last year's slippages; (ii) stresses the need for a timely implementation of all fiscal and governance measures and for enhanced program monitoring; and (ii) urges the authorities to address head-on possible risks to program implementation.

1. INTRODUCTION

1. Discussions on the second review under the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility (PRGF), as wel as on the second annual program under the PRGF arrangement, were held in N'Djamena during November 12-22, 2000 and February 13-28, 2001; technical discussions were concluded in Washington during March 20-23, 2001.' A three-year arrangement under the PRGF was approved by the Executive Board on January 7, 2000 in an amount equivalent to SDR 36.4 million (65 percent of quota).2

2. The Executive Board concluded the 20o0 Article IV consultations and the first review under the PRGF arrangement on July 25, 2000 (EBS/00/133; 7/10/00). On that occasion, Executive Directors noted the authorities' effort to keep the fiscal program on track despite deterioration in the energy situation and underscored the need for strengthening revenue mobilization and expenditure control. They stressed the particular importance of good governance in preparation for the petroleum era. Executive Directors also considered Chad's interim poverty reduction strategy paper (PRSP) and the preliminary document under the enhanced Initiative for Heavily Indebted Poor Countries (HIPC), and declared Chad eligible for assistance under the Initiative.

3. As explained below, fiscal slippages and governance problems resulted in a delay in the completion of the second PRGF review and in presenting Chad's decision point document to the Fund and Bank Executive Boards. Nevertheless, preparations of the fully participatory PRSP proceeded according to the calendar envisaged in the interim PRSP. The Executive Board of the Fund was informed about these developments in the context of informal discussions on country matters on December 11, 20o0 and March 14, 2001.

4. In the attached letter of April 28, 200l (Appendix I), Prime Minister Yamassoum and Finance Minister Louani review developments under the program in 2000 and request waivers for the nonobservance of the performance criteria at end-September 2000 on (i) net credit to the central government; (ii) the current primary fiscal balance; (ii) the net reduction in domestic payments arrears; and (iv) the nonaccumulation of external payments arrears (the latter was breached in December 20o0). The letter also sets out the government's policies and measures to be implemented through end-March 2002, on which basis the government of Chad seeks the Fund's support in the context of a second annual program under the PRGF arrangement. Chad also requests an increase of the PRGF arrangement by SDR 5.6 million (corresponding to 10 percent of quota) to address famine-related food emergencies.

5． Summaries of Chad's relations with the Fund and the World Bank Group are contained in Appendices II and III, respectively. An analysis of governance issues is provided in Appendix IV. Finally, statistical issues are discussed in Appendix V.
I1I. PERFORMANCE UNDER THE PROGRAM IN 2OOO AND RECENT DEVELOPMENTS

6. In 2000, the economic outcome in terms of growth and inflation was close to program projection. Real GDP growth is estimated to have reached 0.6 percent, somewhat below the 1 percent program target, implying a 2 percent decline in per capita terms (Table 3). The energy situation continued to be difficult and supply shortages remained frequent. In addition, the 1999/2000 cotton harvest and food production were considerably weaker than the year earlier, owing to erratic weather conditions; Chad now faces the worst famine in a decade. At the same time, private and public sector investment rose strongly, mainly reflecting activities in the country's two oil fields. On average, consumer prices increased by 3.8 percent in 2000, in line with the program target. Broad money rose by 18.5 percent while velocity declined by over 12 percent, mainly reflecting an increase in money demand with the start of the oil sector projects. According to the Central African Banking Commission (COBAC), the financial sector's health remained good, with only one bank failing to comply with main prudential ratios at end-December 2000. The external current account deficit, excluding official transfers, widened to 16 percent of GDP in 2000 from 15.3 percent in 1999 and against 14.9 percent under the program, reflecting mainly an increase in imports, related to the exploitation of Chad's two oil fields. The curreni account deficit was financed chiefly by foreign direct investment and official loans and grants; net official foreign exchange reserves increased by CFAF 3.4 billion; at end-2000, gross reserves covered 3.7 months of non-pipeline imports. Chad has maintained much of its gains in external competitiveness since the devaluation of the CFA franc in 1994.

7. Against this background, however, the government's record of economic policy implementation has been very uneven. Three periods can be distinguished: (i) a broadly satisfactory performance until mid-2000; (i) starting in mid-2000, policy slippages in the area of transparency and good governance and fiscal policies, as well as in structural reforms; and (ii) a steadily improving track record since November 2000. The first period was discussed in detail on the occasion of the first program review; this report, therefore, focuses on the two subsequent periods.
A. Policy Slippages in the Second Half of 2000

8. Starting in mid-20oo, overall policy implementation weakened across the board. In the area of transparency and governance, contrary to its commitment under the PRGF arrangement, the government did not use a US\$25 million petroleum agreement signing bonus in accordance with the law governing the use of oil revenue on priority sectors, and it did not follow budget procedures. By mid-October 2000, some 60 percent of the bonus had been spent offbudget on nonpriority sectors (of which almost half on defense) (Box 1).
Box 1: Chad: The Case of the Petroleum Agreement Signing Bonus

Following the signing of the agreement with the international consortium that builds the Chad-Cameroon pipeline and exploits the Doba oil field in the south of Chad, the government received $\mathtt{U S S25}$ million (equivalent to CFAF 17 billion, or about 1.7 percent of GDP) as a signing bonus in April 2000. In the context of the PRGF-supported program, the authorities committed themselves to spending this bonus on priority sectors as defined in the Law on Petroleum Revenue Management (health, education, rural development, and basic infrastructure). They also agreed to apply other provisions of the law, which require that (i) spending decisions would be approved by the Oil Revenues Control and Monitoring Board (CCSRP), whose members include representatives of the govermment, parliament, and civil society; (ii) all spending would go through official budget procedures; and (ii the implementation of the law would be monitored by the parliament and the Supreme Court.

In the event, the government started to use the proceeds of the oil bonus without following the above-mentioned requirements. By mid-October 2000, CFAF 10.2 billion had been used, in part on defense, and all off budget, in he following manner:

text: In November 20o0, the government agreed to take several corrective measures concerning the bonus, which constituted prior actions for the presentation of Chad's HIPC decision point document to the Executive Boards of the Fund and the World Bank. These were implemented, and thus the associated prior actions met (see below, and memorandum of economic and financial policies (MEFP), para. 7)

There were other off budget operations, as well as fiscal operations that were executed outside budget procedures and recorded in the fiscal accounts ex post (Box 2). In addition, several major public procurement contracts were granted without competitive bidding. While some of the government's actions might be in part explained by exogenous factors—such as intensified rebel operations in the north, or energy shortages-the overall picture pointed to serious transparency and governance problems.

9. In the area of fiscal policy, at end-September 2000, revenues exceeded the program projection by CFAF 1.9 million (Table 6). However, this good performance was more than offset by higher-than-budgeted current primary expenditures, financed by, inter alia, the signing oil bonus. As a result, the current primary fiscal deficit reached CFAF 7.1 billion (0.9 percent of GDP), as compared with the CFAF 0.7 bilion targeted under the program. The structure of expenditures was also unfavorable: spending on subsidies, transfers, and defense was significantly higher than programmed, while that on priority social sectors was below target. In addition, although the government did not reduce its outstanding domestic arrears as envisaged under the program, net credit to government was above the program target, even if adjusted for the shortfallin foreign financing. Therefore, performance criteria at end-September 2000 were not observed for net credit to government, the current primary balance, and the net reduction of domestic arrears. Around the end of the year, the continuous performance criterion on nonaccumulation of external arrears was also missed (Table 1). The structural reforms process also slowed down in this period (MEFP, para. 5). and none of the structural benchmarks under the program was observed at that time (Table 2). Weak overall performance led to a delay in the completion of the second PRGF review, as well as in reaching the HIPC Initiative decision point.
B. Building a Track Record Since November 2000

10. Against this background, the government took a series of corrective measures in all areas of policy slippage. Transparency and governance have been enhanced by several measures. First, the remainder of the oil bonus remains frozen until the CCSRP becomes fully operational; the government has committed itself to spending the remaining amount in the context of the budget on priority sectors only. Second, the government has issued a public report to the appropriate domestic institutions on the spending of the bonus, and it has committed itself to an audit of the off budget use of the bonus. Third, a Committee on Government Accounts, headed by the Director of the Treasury and co-chaired by the National Director of the BEAC, has reviewed all existing government accounts with the banking sector. On that basis, the Commitee has revised, with technical assistance from the Fund, the list of accounts to be included in the program definition of net credit to government; as a result, the number of govermment accounts included in the new definition has increased from 19 (previous definition) to 76 (Box 2). Finally, the govermment has made a commitment not to engage in offbudget activities, and to follow established budgetary procedures for all expenditure. These measures, which constituted prior actions for the presentation of Chad's HIPC Initiative decision point document to the Executive Boards of the Fund and the World Bank, have been met.
Box 2. Chad: Off-Budget Operations and Revision of Government Accounts

Off budget operations amounted to about 5 percent of GDP in 2000 and covered two main operations: (i) spending of the oil bonus; and (i) investment in the Sedigi oil field, financed by loans from the government of Taiwan Province of China (Box 3). These operations have now been included in the budget, with a commitment by the government not to engage in off budget operations in the future. There were other expenditures, which bypassed regular budget procedures and were recorded in the fiscal accounts only ex post.

As part of the HIPC Initiative decision point prior actions, the government created a high-level Committee on Government Accounts to review, with technical assistance from the Fund, all government accounts and to update the list of accounts that are included in the program definition of net credit to government. As a result of this work, the coverage of the definition of net credit to government was extended from 19 accounts to 76 accounts.This change has also been reflected in the monetary survey (Tables 8a and 8b). The committee continues its work to consolidate its accounts by closing dormant or unused accounts with commercial banks.

11. Fiscal performance was to be improved by measures aimed at both strictly controlling expenditure and at increasing revenue (MEFP, para. 8). All measures were introduced, albeit sometimes with delays. As a result, while the worsening of the fiscal situation was arrested, performance at end-December still fell short of target. The current primary deficit, excluding elections, reached 0.8 percent of GDP, compared with the original program target of a surplus of 0.4 percent of GDP. At the same time, a combination of a tight fiscal situation and major external debt payments faling due has led, for the first time in many years, to an accumulation of external payment arrears. These reached US\$7.7 million by end-March 20o1. The authorities have agreed with most concerned creditors on a plan to settle all arrears according to a time-bound schedule, and agreements with remaining creditors are expected to be completed prior to the Executive Board meeting.

12. Structural reforms were accelerated, achieving a full catch-up by meeting the structural benchmarks missed at end-September. Specifically, (i) a tracking mechanism was adopted to better monitor commitment and actual expenditures in the education and health sectors; (ii) the contract for the general controller of COTONTCHAD was awarded; and (iii) the census of all civil servants was completed and an update of the payroll was launched.
III. THE MEDIUM-TERM FRAMEWORK

13. The revised medium-term framework is premised on the assumptions that (i) the construction of the Chad-Cameroon pipeline and the exploitation of the Doba and Sedigi petroleum fields proceed according to schedule; (ii) prudent fiscal policies are maintained; and (ii) structural reforms are sustained as discussed below. After a decline in 2000, the terms of trade would improve significantly in 200l (as export cotton price increases and the oil price declines), stabilizing afterward. These assumptions are consistent with Chad's poverty reduction strategy as presented in the interim PRSP. Under the above conditions, Chad could start experiencing annual growth rates of about 9 percent (or $6\%$ percent in per capita terms) during the period 200l-03, considerably higher than estimated in the interim PRSP and the original program (4 percent). The revision is due both to an upward revision for oil sector investment, financed chiefly by higher foreign direct investment, and a higher estimate for the associated spillover effects on the economy. Foreign savings (exciuding debt relief) will increase dramatically from 16 percent of GDP in 2000 to 4l percent and to 44 percent of GDP in 2001 and 2002, respectively, reflecting massive inflows of foreign direct investment associated with the petroleum reserve exploitation and pipeline projects. Gross domestic savings will reduce somewhat from 1.6 percent of GDP in 2000 to 0.5 percent of GDP in 2001 before rising to about 4 percent in 2002-03, reflecting movements in government savings. Gross domestic investment is expected to rise sharply from 17 percent of GDP in 2000 to 41 percent in 2001 and further to 47 percent of GDP and 51 percent in 2002 and 2003, respectively, as private investment in the petroleum sector, as well as in construction and related services, increases.
Box 3. Chad: Oil Development Projects
Chad's two main proven oil reserves

There are two ongoing oil development projects in Chad: the smallSedigi project, and the large Doba project, both in theSouthern part of Chad.The proven reserves of the Sedigi field are 20million barrels; the total cost of the project is estimated at $\mathrm{USS40}$ million, financed by a concessional loan from the government of Taiwan Province of China.The project, consisting of drillng and building a pipeline from the field to N'Djamena, is scheduled to be completed by the end of 2001.The production from Sedigi is expected to cover most of the country's domestic needs for petroleum products. As a by-product, the Sedigi oil production would also generate an important amount of gas, whose exploitation is under discussion.

Proven reserves of the Doba field are 883 million barrels.The project has two main components:(i) the“field system,"which includes the drilling of some 300 well and the construction of associated facilities and infrastructure; and (iithe“export system,"consisting of the construction of a 1,070 kilometer long pipeline from the Doba oil field to Cameroon's Atlantic coast, installation of an offshore floating storage and offloading vesselandrelated facilities,and the construction of a smallsubmarine pipeline from Atlantic coastline to the vessel. The total project cost is estimated at $\mathrm{US}\$3.7$ billion. The US\$1.5 billion cost of the field system is financed entirely by a private international consortium of three companies (Exxon, Chevron,and Petronas).The $\mathrm{US}\$2.2$ billion cost of the export system is financed by two joint-venture companies, TOTCO in Chad and COTCO in Cameroon, owned by the international oil consortium and the governments of Chad and Cameroon. These companies willown and operate,respectively, the Chad and Cameron portions of the export system. The governments of Chad and Cameroon have obtained loans from the World Bank (US\$39.5 million and US\$53.4 million, respectively) to help finance the export system. The total investments for both components to be made in Chad are estimated at $\mathbf{US}\pmb{\Omega}.1$ billion. The construction and development phase will take about four years, and oil exports are expected to begin in 2005.
Main impact of the oil projects

? Economic growth.Real GDP in 20ol-03 will receive a boost from investment in the two oil fields and its spillover effects on other sectors, mostly services. Of the total projected real GDP growth of about 9 percent per annum in this period, about two thirds is explained by the oil projects. Budget.Taxation of the Sedigi field willbe developed with technical assistance from the Fund and the World Bank by September 2001, so that the adopted plan would be incorporated in the 2002 budget. Preliminary estimates indicate oil revenues of 0.5-0.8 percent of GDP per annum. The revenue stream from the Doba field willbe managed according to the above-mentioned Law on Petroleum Revenue Management to benefit mainly priority social sectors. It is expected that, starting in 2005, oil revenues will amount to 3-4 percentage points of GDP per annum. Poverty.Most of the poverty-reducing impact of the oil projects willmaterialize following the start of oil production in the Doba field starting at end-2004, through three main channels.First, oil revenues will permit large increases in spending on health and education. Second, spending on rural development and basic infrastructure willhelp eliminating major impediments to growth in rural areas. Third, growth will generate employment and thus higher revenue for poor households. External accounts. In the period ahead, the external current account, excluding grants, will widen sharply from 16 percent of GDP in 2000 to 41 percent in 2001, to 44 percent in 2002, and to 47 percent in 2003; imports of goods and services related to oil projects account for two thirds of the increase in the deficit in 2001 and for all the increase in 2002-03.
IV. THE SECOND ANNUAL PROGRAM UNDER THE THREE-YEAR PRGF ARRANGEMENT \$
A. Macroeconomic Policies

14. The second annual program has two key policy components: credible fiscal adjustment and strong measures to improve governance further. In addition, the pace of structural reforms will be sustained. A substantial fiscal adjustment effort is designed to achieve fiscal viability. The program's governance element aims at enhancing transparency and accountability. The structural part focuses on public resource management (enhancing revenue collection and expenditure management and control), as well as on the liberalization of domestic petroleum prices. Other main elements of the government's structural program-civil service reform, reform of the procurement system and of the cotton sector are being covered by World Bank conditionality.

15.As mentioned, macroeconomic objectives under the second annual program are in line with those outlined in Chad's interim PRSP, including sustained high rates of economic growth, spearheaded by oil sector-related private investment in a low-inflation environment. Specific quantified targets for the new annual program differ somewhat from those envisaged in the interim PRSP to take account of the latest economic developments. These revisions, which are being incorporated in the macroeconomic framework prepared for the full PRSP, pertain mostly to higher real growth and wider current account deficit projections stemming from the dynamism of oil-sector investment and its spillover effects. The PRGF-supported program also incorporates the preliminary results of the participatory consultations that indicate that Chad's poverty reduction strategy will have an increased focus on governance.
Fiscal policy

16.The underlying fiscal position-—measured by the current primary fiscal balance, excluding exceptional famine and election-related spending, the mainly foreign-financed investment, and Poverty Fund-financed outlays--is targeted to turn from a deficit of 0.8 percent of GDP in 2000 into a surplus of 0.2 percent of GDP in 2001. Nevertheless, owing to exceptional as well as specific poverty-related spending and high public investment, the overall fiscal balance, on a commitment basis and excluding grants, will widen from 12.3 percent of GDP in 2000 to 20.8 percent in 2001.6

17.Fiscal revenues are targeted to increase from 8 percent of GDP in 2000 to 8.4 percent in 2001 (equivalent to a 12 percent increase in real terms). This increase will be achieved by, in addition to the above described measures introduced in November 20o0, the introduction of additional measures to enhance revenue collection, including, inter alia, interest payments on government deposits with commercial banks, nongranting of“exceptional" customs duty exemptions, and measures to reinforce customs administration, including the latter's audit.

18.As a share of GDP, current primary expenditure, as defined above, is projected to fall from 8.8 percent of GDP in 2000 to 8.2 percent in 2001. The nonmilitary wage bill is projected to increase by 9.5 percent in 200l, owing to'an increase in the wage bill of the priority social sectors. The military wage bill will rise by about 13 percent to include a cost of living adjustment, which has not been changed for three years. Total defense spending is budgeted to rise slightly to 1.6 percent of GDP. The government is preparing an action plan to reduce domestic arrears. It has already regularized part of its arrears toward state-owned enterprises; it is now in the process of verifying the remaining stock of arrears with a view to establishing a timetable for their settlement. The government has also identified possible expenditure cuts in nonpriority sectors should there be a shortfall in revenues, targeting nonwage current and investment spending in non-social ministries (internal security, Ministry of Finance, etc.)

19. Exceptional spending for food emergencies and elections will reach one percentage point of GDP in 20o1. The government will provide an amount equivalent to 0.6 percent of GDP (CFAF 7.2 billon) to address food emergency needs, of which CFAF 5.1 billion will be financed by the requested SDR 5.6 million increase of the PRGF arrangement.7 Election costs are estimated at 0.4 percent of GDP, part of which is being covered by donor support. Public investment (excluding spending from the Poverty Fund) is projected to rise strongly from 10.3 percent of GDP in 2000 to 17.7 percent in 2001, reflecting the completion of the pipeline construction from the Sedigi oil field to N'Djamena and projects financed by loans from the multilateral institutions, including the part of the Cameroon-Chad oil pipeline financed by the World Bank.

20. The government is establishing a virtual Poverty Fund (PF) to help achieve its poverty-reducing objectives, as laid down in Chad's interim PRSP. The PF will also facilitate the fulfillment of the floating point conditions for the HIPC Initiative completion point. Initially, the government had intended to fund the PF only by resources from HIPC Initiative assistance; however, following the above described unfortunate use of the oil bonus in 2000, it has decided to augment the PF's resources by the remainder of the oil bonus in 2001. Total PF resources will, therefore, reach CFAF 14.9 billion in 2001 (1.3 percent of GDP); of which 60 percent from HIPC interim assistance and 40 percent from the remainder of the oil bonus. As described in MEFP paragraphs 3O-31, the PF will be fully integrated into the budget.

text: Monetary prospects and financial sector issues

21. Monetary policy will continue to be conducted at the regional level by the BEAC, covering member states of the Economic and Monetary Community of Central Africa (CEMAC). The BEAC's key objective is to maintain price stability over the medium term by maintaining the peg between the CFA franc and the euro, and to build up the monetary zone's official reserves. It is expected that, reflecting the carryover of energy price increases in 2000 and the weak food supply,consumer prices in Chad willincrease by about 7 percent on average in 2001, and that money supply will grow by about 19 percent. Banking supervision will continue to be strengthened by the COBAC; the capital of the commercial bank, which failed to comply with prudential regulations at end-20oo, is expected to be increased by June 200l. The government supports efforts to adopt a unified banking licensing system within the CEMAC, and to establish a regional capital market and adopt a common legal framework for microfinance.
External sector

22.The external current account deficit, excluding official transfers, is projected to widen from 16 percent of GDP in 2000 to 41 percent in 2001, of which 17 percentage points are related to imports associated with oil sector investment. Exports in value terms will rise by close to 8 percent, as a decline in volumes of cotton exports will be more than offset by an expected 15 percent increase in export cotton prices. Imports in value terms are projected to more than double, mainly reflecting the increase in oil project related imports; other imports will also rise as a result of an increase in emergency food imports and in foreign-financed public investment programs (Table 7).

23. Much of the current account deficit will be financed by expected foreign direct investment (including the Cameroon-Chad pipeline project) and sizable project-related loans. Nevertheless, after the assumed Fund disbursement (including the abovementioned 10 percent increase in quota access), there remains a financing gap of CFAF 48.7 billion (4.3 percent of GDP). This gap is expected to be covered by support under assistance from the World Bank (CFAF 17.4 billion), the European Union (CFAF 14.3 billion), and the African Development Bank (CFAF 4 billion), as well as by bilateral donors and expected external debt rescheduling (for a total of CFAF 12.9 billion).
HIPC Initiative assistance

24.An update of the external debt sustainability analysis using end-200o data has resulted in an increase in external debt relief (in net present value (NPV) terms) from $\mathrm{\US\$157}$ million (based on end-1999 data included in the preliminary HIPC Initiative document)° to US\$170 million, reflecting a combination of exchange rate movements and somewhat lower (three-year moving average) exports. Interim HIPC initiative assistance will reach US\$12.4 million (0.9 percent of GDP) in 2001 and US\$15.7 million (1 percent of GDP) in 2002 (Table 10). The planned use of these resources, as well as the tracking mechanism to monitor all poverty related expenditure through a virtual Poverty Fund, are summarized in Box 4, and described in paragraph 19, in MEFP, paras. 30 and 31.9

text: B. Governance10

25.The program's governance package is key to reestablishing credibility. The envisaged measures focus on establishing accountability in public resource management and enhancing existing institutions and instruments that affect governance directly. Accountability will be enhanced by a number of audits: (i) the audit of the use of the oil bonus, as of end-December, 2000, by the Accounting Office (Chambre des Comptes) of the Supreme Court; the audit will be completed by September 30, 2001; (ii) a functional and financial audit of customs administration; and (ii) audits of the five largest public

procurement contracts granted in 200l, other than those financed and monitored by the World Bank, the African Development Bank and the European Union. Audits mentioned in (ii) and (ii) willbe carried out with financial support from the World Bank. The results of the audits will be made public and the associated follow-up measures will be integrated into the overall reform of public procurement, to be carried out with assistance by the World Bank. Accountability will be also improved by including all stakeholders in the defining and monitoring of Chad's governance strategy, which is expected to be adopted in June 2001."

26. Existing institutions will be enhanced by : (i) specific measures in the context of the civil service reform (adoption by the parliament of the new civil service statutes and the computerization of the harmonized civil service and payroll file); and (ii) in the context of the public procurement reform (see below), publication in June 200l of the first quarterly public procurement bulletin, including a list of firms barred from bidding. These measures will be implemented with assistance from the World Bank. Furthermore, the government will accelerate implementation of the judicial reform through, inter alia, the setting up of appellate and commercial courts.
C. Structural Policies

27. In the structural area, the program focuses on structural fiscal policies and price liberalization of domestic petroleum products. The government plans to press ahead with reforms in other areas (civil service, procurement system, cotton sector (MEFP, paras. 25- 27)); these areas are being covered by World Bank conditionality.

28. Structural fiscal policies will aim at strengthening both revenue collection and expenditure management. Revenue collection measures include : (i) improving VAT administration, with Fund technical assistance; (ii) reinforcing the joint tax and customs brigades by, inter alia, providing for additional transportation equipment; (iii) completing the assignment to all economic agents of the personal tax identification number (NIF) by endJune 2001 and excluding firms without a NIF from public procurement bidding, as well as from any supply and payment orders; (iv) completing the computerization of the customs database via the use of SYDONIA; and (v) computerizing the large taxpayer unit by October 2001.

29.Appropriately centralized expenditure control is a precondition to the successful implementation of the program. The main measures include : (i) avoiding off budget operations, starting in 201; (ii) extending the coverage of the treasury cash-flow plan to 12 months, with a regular update of at least twice a month, starting on April 1, 2001 (see below); (ii) simplifying the expenditure circuit adopted by a decree in December 2000 and January 200l through, inter alia, the publication of a new procedure handbook by May 30, 2001, and the computerization of the expenditure circuit by December 2001, with technical assistance from the World Bank; (iv) ensuring that the treasury will receive information on the execution of allpublic investment projects from the project managers on a monthly basis; (v) granting, starting on March 1, 2001, an exclusive mandate to the Minister of Finance (in his absence, the Director of the Treasury) to authorize the opening of a bank account lodging government resources; and (vi) consolidating the government's accounts with commercial banks by eliminating inactive accounts. In addition, a study will be conducted on the guidelines for reforming budget elaboration, monitoring, and regulation procedures by September 30, 2001, with the assistance from the Fund and the World Bank.

30. After years of hesitation that have led to persistent shortages in energy supplies and in the supply of petroleum products at fixed offcial prices, as well as to soaring prices in flourishing informal markets, the government has decided to liberalize the domestic price of petroleum products.'2 To this end, it has submitted to parliament a draft law liberalizing the domestic prices of petroleum products (a prior action under the program, see below). A joint committee in charge of monitoring domestic prices of petroleum products will follow price movements on a regular basis, with a view to ensuring that the market remains competitive.
D. The PRSP Process

31. Following the endorsement of the interim PRSP by the Executive Boards of the Fund and the World Bank in July 20o0, the authorities have been proceeding with the preparation of a fully participatory poverty reduction strategy according to schedule. The process of moving toward the full PRSP consists of three phases: the diagnostic, setting of strategic objectives, and implementation phases. The diagnostic phase, which aimed at deepening the understanding of the various dimensions of poverty in Chad, was completed in January 2001. Based on the information gathered from the several activities undertaken during this period with assistance from development partners-a survey on the perception of welfare and poverty, sectoral studies, a civil society forum, participatory consultations, and the analysis of a few representative poverty-reducing projects-—, the Steering Committeel3 has prepared a first draft of the PRSP. This tentatively identifies eight strategic objectives underpinning the overall poverty reduction goal: (i) sustainable economic growth; (ii) food security; (ii) health; (iv) education; (v) good governance; (vi) mobility and living conditions (especially housing); (vii) protection of the environment; and (vii) close monitoring of developments in poverty.

32. The second phase of the process, which aims at finalizing the definition of the strategic objectives and priority actions to achieve them, will be completed by early June 2001. During this phase, a series of participatory seminars are being held in N'Djamena and in the provinces with the objective of discussing the first draft of the PRSP. The third-the implementation-phase willstart in early summer, during which the costing of the identified priority actions will be carried out, with a view to integrating them into the 2002 budget. The PRSP is expected to be finalized by the fall of 2001.
E. Data Issues

33. Chad continues to suffer from significant weaknesses in its statistical system (Appendix V). A multisectoral STA mission in May 2000 proposed wide-ranging improvements to strengthen the statistical database, particularly in the area of national income accounts, balance of payments, and government finance. The government has started to implement these recommendations; however, progress has been limited, particularly in the area of government finance. Improvements in the latter area nevertheless occurred, following the revision of monetary accounts and the commitment made by the government not to engage in off budget activities. In addition, the government willlaunch a household survey on income and expenditure in May 200l, with assistance from the World Bank. The results of the survey-the definition of poverty line and the establishment of main poverty indicators (incidence, depth, and severity)-will be continuously fed into the national PRS.14
F. Prior Actions, Fund Conditionality, and Program Monitoring

34.Three prior actions have been set with the objective of ensuring that conditions conducive to the program's success are in place at the time of the program's consideration by the Executive Board. First, a modified budget law for 2001 is to be submitted to parliament; this will also include the government's program regarding the use of external debt relief under the enhanced HIPC Initiative and the modalities of the use of the remainder of the oil bonus. Second, the coverage of the monthly cashflow plan is to be extended to 12 months. Third, a draft law is to be presented to parliament with the objective to liberalize domestic prices of petroleum products. These prior actions are presented in the MEFP, para. 32.

35.Program conditionality has been streamlined to cover only areas that are directly relevant, and often critical, to the program's success. Quantitative performance criteria are set to allow for the appropriate monitoring of the implementation of macroeconomic policies under the program, and consist of targets for (i) fiscal performance (net credit to the government, the fiscal revenue, the current primary fiscal balance, and the nonaccumulation of external payments arrears);'5 and (ii) external debt sustainability (limits on nonconcessional borrowing). In order to follow pro-poor expenditure more closely, indicative minimum targets are set for education and health current spending; a ceiling on the government's total wage bill is also set, with a view to ensuring the attainment of the program's fiscal objectives. Conditionality in the structural area focuses on governance and oil taxation issues, given their direct relevance to fiscal sustainability and overall program implementation. All other structural areas-—civil service, procurement, and cotton sector reforms—are being covered by World Bank conditionality (MEFP paras. 24-26).1% As the government's commitment to improve governance is critical to the successful implementation of the new program, a structural performance criterion is set on the completion of the audit on the off budget use of the oil bonus. Structural benchmarks are established to cover other areas that are important for the success of the program (improving governance (launching of the customs audit), as wellas achieving the fiscal objectives (the payment of interest on government accounts and the undertaking of a study on the pricing and taxation of the Sedigi oil field)). Quantitative and structural performance criteria and structural benchmarks are summarized in Tables 1 and 2 of the authorities' MEFP.

36. While the second annual program will continue to include semiannual reviews, it will provide for quarterly performance criteria and associated disbursements, with a view to ensuring effective and timely program monitoring (Table 9). The third review will be completed by end-December 200l, and will focus on budget implementation and governance issues.
V. STAFF APPRAISAL

37. Chad has recently emerged from a difficult, albeit relatively short, period of policy slippages. The first test of the use of oil revenues--the $\mathbf{US}\$25$ million signing bonus--was not passed successfully, as some 60 percent of the amount was used outside the budget and not in conformity with the law on oil revenue management. Fiscal policies were also relaxed, and the implementation of structural reforms slowed. However, the government drew the appropriate lessons from this experience and has introduced a series of corrective measures since November 2ooo in order to build a good track record. Its renewed

commitment to pursue sound macroeconomic policies and break with past flawed governance practices has been demonstrated, inter alia, by meeting prior actions for the HIPC Initiative decision point and the program, including the bold decision to liberalize domestic petroleum prices and the decisive actions to clean up fiscal and government bank accounts.

38. The second annual PRGF-supported program is appropriately ambitious and, if fully implemented, should strengthen private sector confidence considerably on the eve of the petroleum era. Indeed, the government can ill afford the repetition of the mistakes of the recent past without jeopardizing the historic opportunity of exploiting the country's natural wealth. A vigorous implementation of the program, therefore, is essential; this will be helped by an enhanced quarterly program monitoring that will enable the authorities to address, jointly with staff, any problem without costly delays.

39.Key to fiscal consolidation are the introduction of revenue measures, including the commitment not to grant any exceptional exemptions from customs duties, and the strict implementation of measures to control expenditure. The monthly treasury cashflow plan, now extended to cover 12 months, can serve as a main instrument for short-term cash planning and the monitoring of budget implementation; the simplification of the expenditure circuit and the establishment of the virtual Poverty Fund will help ensure better tracking of poverty-reducing spending. Finally, treasury management will be enhanced by, inter alia, consolidating the large number of government accounts in commercial banks, as well as by granting an exclusive mandate to the Minister of Finance to authorize the opening of a government bank account.

40. In light of recent problems, the program puts appropriately large emphasis on further improving governance. The timely execution of the audit of the off budget use of the oil bonus and of customs administration will go a long way toward establishing an accountability of civil servants, including at higher levels. The staff also welcomes the government's effort to involve civil society in developing its long-term strategy to improve governance.

41. The authorities have made good progress toward preparing a fully participatory PRSP. The staff recommends that the authorities start preparing the costing of the identified measures and actions without delay, so that they can be incorporated in the 2002 budget. The authorities are also to be commended for augmenting, in 2oo1, the resources of the virtual Poverty Fund with the remainder of the oil bonus. This action will help Chad meet the floating point conditions for the HIPC completion point.

42. Despite recent improvements in the government's track record, program implementation still faces several risks. First, Chad is in the middle of a long election period, during which additional spending pressures, including for wages, are likely to intensify. Second, while presently under control, the security situation in the north may deteriorate with renewed rebel fighting, necessitating increases in defense spending. Finaly, there might be a temptation to mortgage Chad's future oil revenues to ease fiscal

pressures in the run-up to the petroleum era. It is imperative that the government prepare for such eventualities: (i) spending pressures in the run-up to elections must continue to be resisted; (i) any additional defense spending will have to be fully and transparently budgeted, with offsetting measures introduced outside the priority social sectors; and (iii) the costly and inherently nontransparent mortgaging of the country's future oil revenue must be avoided. In this regard, the staff urges the authorities to adhere to their commitment made in the context of the IBRD pipeline loan agreement.

43. In view of the progress made to date, including the authorities' actions in building a track record, as well as the appropriate policies envisaged for 200l, the staff recommends that the second review under the PRGF arrangement be completed, with waivers granted for the nonobservance of performance criteria, and that the authorities' request for a second annual PRGF-supported program be approved. In light of the threat of famine, the staff also recommends the approval of Chad's request for an increase of the PRGF arrangement by SDR 5.6 million (corresponding to 10 percent of quota), to be disbursed upon the approval by the Executive Board of the second annual program.
VI. PROPOSED DECISION
The following draft decision is proposed for adoption by the Executive Board:

1. Chad has consulted with the Fund in accordance with paragraph 2(ee) of the threeyear arrangement for Chad under the Poverty Reduction and Growth Facility (PRGF) (EBS/99/234, Supplement 1, 01/11/00, as amended) and paragraph 4 of the letter from the Minister of Finance of Chad dated July 6, 2000 in order to review program implementation and reach understandings regarding the phasing and conditions for disbursements during the remainder of the second year of the arrangement.

2. The letter dated April 28, 2001 from the Prime Minister and the Minister of Finance of Chad (the“Letter"), together with the attached Memorandum of Economic and Financial Policies (the “Memorandum") and the atached Technical Memorandum of Understanding (the “TMU"), shall be attached to the three-year PRGF arrangement for Chad, and the letters dated November 12, 1999 and July 6, 2000, together with their respective Memoranda of Economic and Financial Policies, shall be read as supplemented and modified by the letter dated April 28, 2001 from the Prime Minister and the Minister of Finance of Chad.
3. Accordingly, the three-year PRGF arrangement for Chad shall be amended as follows:

a. In paragraph 1(a) “SDR 36.4 million" shall be replaced by “SDR 42 million". b. In paragraph 1 (b) “SDR 20.8 million" shall be replaced by “SDR 23.8 million". c. Paragraph 1(cc) shall be amended to add “(i)" before “During" and
“SDR 5.2 million" shall be replaced by “SDR 10.8 million".
d. The following sentence shall be added to paragraph 1(cc):

"(ii) The fourth disbursement, in an amount equivalent to SDR 2.6 million, will be made available after September 30, 2001, at the request of Chad and subject to paragraph 2 below; and (iii) the fifth disbursement, in an amount equivalent to SDR 2.6 million, will be made available after December 31, 2001, at the request of Chad and subject to paragraph 2 below."

， The following sentence shall be added to paragraph 1(dd): "The phasing of, and the conditions for, disbursements during the third year of the arrangement shall be established in the context of the third review of Chad's program with the Trustee contemplated in paragraph 7 of the Letter.
f. Paragraph 2 shall be revised to read as follows:
“2. Chad shall not request the fourth and fifth disbursements provided for in paragraph 1(cc):

(a) If the Managing Director of the Trustee finds that with regard to the fourth disbursement data as of June 30, 20ol, and with regard to the fifth disbursement, data as of September 30, 2001, indicate that
(i) the ceiling on the increase in net credit of the banking system to the central government, or

(ii) the floor on current primary fiscal balance, or (iii) the floor on total government revenue excluding grants, or (iv) the ceiling on new nonconcessional external debt with maturities of more than one year contracted or guaranteed by the central government, or

(V) the ceiling on net change in external debt with a maturity of up to and including one year, except normal trade financing, s set out in Table 1 of the Memorandum and further specified in the TMU was not observed; or

(b) if the Managing Director of the Trustee finds, with regard to the fifth disbursement, that Chad has not carried out its intentions by September 30, 2001 with respect to the completion of the audit on the use of the oil bonus as of December 31, 2000, as set forth in Table 2 of the Memorandum..."
g. The following sentence shall be added to paragraph 2(ee):

"Chad shall not request the disbursement of the fifth loan referred to in paragraph 1(cc) above until the Trustee has determined that the third review referred to in paragraph 7 of the Letter has been completed."
4. The Fund decides that:
(a) the second review contemplated in paragraph 2(ee) of the three-year PRGF arrangement for Chad is completed; and

(b) Chad may request the disbursement of the third loan under the arrangement referred to in paragraph 1(cc) of the three-year PRGF arrangement for Chad, notwithstanding the non-observance of the end-September 2000 quantitative performance criteria on net domestic bank credit to the government, current primary fiscal surplus, and net reduction in domestic payments arrears of the central government specified in paragraphs 2(a)(i) to (iii) of the arrangement and the performance criterion nonaccumulation of external payments arrears specified in

paragraph 2(c), on the condition that the information provided by Chad on the observance of these performance criteria and on the implementation of the measures specified in paragraph 32 of the Memorandum is accurate.
